Headline: Chips, Geopolitics, and Global Infrastructures
Text: Instead of presenting a complete history of semiconductors, this timeline examines the turning points that transformed chips from scientific discoveries into the infrastructure underpinning global power. It highlights how technological innovation became inseparable from labour, environmental resources, geopolitical competition, and state intervention.

Headline: The Transistor
Text: The invention of the transistor at Bell Laboratories marked the beginning of modern electronics. Beyond replacing vacuum tubes, it established research laboratories as strategic sites where scientific innovation, military investment, and industrial development became closely connected.
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: Integrated Circuit
Text: Jack Kilby's integrated circuit radically reduced the size and cost of electronic systems. Cold War military funding accelerated its adoption, demonstrating how public investment helped shape the semiconductor industry long before consumer electronics emerged.
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: Gordon Moore Formulates His Famous "Law"
Text: Moore's Law became less a physical law than an industrial roadmap. Continuous miniaturisation drove innovation but also encouraged rapid product replacement, increasing material extraction and electronic waste worldwide.

Headline: Globalisation of Assembly
Text: Semiconductor companies increasingly relocated labour-intensive assembly and packaging to Southeast Asia. This reorganisation created global supply chains based on geographical inequalities in labour costs while concentrating design and intellectual property in the United States.
Group: The 70s and 80s: The Birth of the Personal Computer

Headline: The TSMC Revolution
Text: The creation of TSMC introduced the pure-play foundry model, separating chip design from manufacturing. Taiwan consequently became indispensable to the global semiconductor supply chain, turning fabrication capacity into a geopolitical asset.
Group: The 90s: The Era of Multimedia PCs and Pure Foundry

Headline: The Thermal Wall
Text: As processors approached physical thermal limits, the semiconductor industry shifted from increasing clock frequency to multi-core architectures. The challenge was no longer only computational performance but also energy efficiency, anticipating the rapid growth of data-centre electricity consumption.
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: Smartphone Revolution
Text: The launch of the iPhone symbolised the transition to mobile computing. Smartphones dramatically increased demand for rare-earth elements, cobalt, tantalum, and advanced semiconductor manufacturing, connecting everyday consumer electronics with global mining and environmental pressures.
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: Vertical Transistors (FinFET)
Text: FinFET transistors extended Moore's Law but required increasingly complex fabrication plants. Advanced semiconductor manufacturing became dependent on stable electricity supplies, ultra-pure water, and highly specialised cleanroom infrastructure, making environmental resources central to technological progress.
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: Global Chip Shortage and Sovereign Disruptions
Text: Pandemic disruptions exposed the fragility of global semiconductor supply chains. Governments recognised that semiconductor production had become essential infrastructure for economic security, healthcare, transportation, and defence.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers

Headline: Export Controls
Text: Export restrictions on advanced AI chips demonstrate that semiconductors have become instruments of geopolitical power. Access to leading-edge processors is increasingly regulated through national security policies rather than market competition alone.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers

Headline: Sub-3nm Fabs and Environmental Resource Strains
Text: Advanced 2 nm semiconductor manufacturing marks a new phase in the global competition for technological leadership. Cutting-edge fabrication plants require enormous investments, highly specialised infrastructure, ultra-pure water, and reliable energy supplies. As governments seek to strengthen domestic semiconductor production through industrial policies such as the CHIPS Acts, environmental sustainability and strategic resilience have become inseparable from technological innovation.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers
